feedback.text: I would like to bring to the EUs attention the movement of Stop Killing Games, something that works to preserve the rights of consumers to ensure they have access to games they have paid money for in some way or another in a manner that remains accessible. For far too long, game companies have had the free reign to willingly and, most of the time, deliberately remove access from people who have paid for a game by forcing them to be online only or through servers only accessible through them. Games I have paid for such as Wildstar, a Massively Multiplayer Online Roleplaying Game (MMORPG for short), and Overwatch, a first person shooting with heroic characters, have in recent years gone completely offline with no ability to access or play them - with Overwatch being completely replaced by its sequel. Blizzard Entertainment had also recently, by releasing the "remaster" of its tactical game Warcraft 3 aimed to completely erase the original from players being able to access, a game which prior to the remaster release was playable for 20 years. A game completely replaced by a better looking but functionally inferior product. As a consumer located within the EU, watching as American and Chinese companies create games and ask me to pay for them upfront or for playable characters through predatory Gacha systems and then remove my ability to play what I paid for is insulting. One example of one closer to home is with Ubisoft - a game company based in Montreuil, France - that has activately lauded over players the concept of "You should just get used to not owning your games", boasting that they have every right to just turn them off and you effectively having had your money stolen from you. Additionally with Ubisoft, they have - alongside their lobbying partners who have the same mindset of video game destruction - intentionally mischaracterized the Stop Killing Games movement by presenting points they do not make, as well as blatantly lying about some points about game development in order to make it seem that SKG would limit developer choice - something that is caused by the people in charge of companies and appealing to shareholders to maximize profits, not the idea of having an end of life plan for a game. Many of these games are simply turned off and removed from the consumer to access through the model of Live service, where players will often pay to play the game upfront (which is steadily becoming more expensive) and pay for extra microtransactions ingame - most of these would be from EA and Microsoft - or via Free to Play games which have several microtransactions convince you to pay. Many of these live service games will have popups and adverts in menus reminding you of things available to buy. Games that would eventually be removed from being accessible at the end of their life. Stop Killing Games is a movement looking to preserve the rights of consumers being able to access future games by having developers prepare end of life plans which include but are not limited to allowing the consumers to make their own servers and keep the games running themselves without legal repercussions - so long as said consumers do not attempt to sell access to these games themselves. I implore you to truly engage with reading through and understanding the Stop Killing Games movement and consider putting the consumer first in manners of games preservation, rather than allow the companies that create these games to tell you "its impossible to do this" with honeyed words. Thank you for taking the time to read this.
